result:
The first thing I notice when I wake up is the sound the house makes at 5:47 a.m.—a sort of low, respiratory hum that might be the refrigerator or might be the joists remembering last night’s wind. I’ve never decided. I lie still and let the sound enter me the way a tide slips into a cave, slow and inevitable, until my own pulse synchronizes with it. For a few seconds I am a rented room inside a larger lung. Then the neighbor’s dog barks once, a clean puncture, and the spell is broken. I become a person again, with a name and a to-do list and a slightly stiff left shoulder from sleeping with one arm pinned under the pillow like a smuggled object.

I make coffee the way my grandfather taught me: wet the filter first, wake the grounds with a dribble of water, let them bloom like shy creatures before you drown them. He used a Melitta cone balanced on a jelly jar, and though I now own gleaming appliances that could wake me with Bluetooth espresso, I still set the plastic cone on a mason jar every morning, the way a pilgrim carries a river stone from shrine to shrine. The coffee drips through in dark rosary beads. While it drains, I open the back door and let the night air finish leaving. Sometimes it takes a minute, sometimes an hour. There is no predicting the etiquette of darkness.

When I was eight I believed the backyard was a country. I had a plywood table under the maple where I issued visas to beetles and stamped their elytra with dots from a three-hole punch. Citizenship was inexpensive: a dead leaf or a fragment of eggshell got you full rights, no questions asked. I kept census scrolls in an old Band-Aid tin. One afternoon my mother mowed the lawn while I was at school, and when I came home the entire nation had been guillotined. I stood on the stubble where the parliament of dandelions used to meet and felt the first vertigo of history: the way a world can end not with thunder but with the mild perfume of cut grass. That night I buried the Band-Aid tin under the forsythia. I imagine it’s still down there, a metal womb preserving the names of beetles who never knew they were expatriates.

I’ve been trying to write a novel for twelve years. It started as a short story about a woman who loses her reflection and ends up joining a traveling circus of similarly afflicted people. The ringmaster has two shadows; the acrobat casts none at all; the fortune-teller can only scry in other people’s bathroom mirrors. Every time I open the file it has grown new limbs. One draft ends with a flood, one with a fire, one with the entire midway lifted into the sky by a balloon made of wedding dresses. The woman—her name keeps changing—keeps insisting she isn’t interested in plot, only in the moment the glass goes blank. I understand her completely. Some days I open the document, add a single comma, then close it again, like a caretaker adjusting the tilt of a picture frame in an abandoned museum.

We are told to write what we know, but what I know is the smell of library dust at closing time, the way my father’s voice cracked only once—on the word “goodbye”—the precise weight of a newborn niece against my collarbone. None of these arrives with instructions for use. They accumulate like the ceramic shards archaeologists call “potsherds,” evidence of vessels whose shapes we can only guess. I keep them in a mental shoebox labeled “Probably Important,” and when I reach in I inevitably cut myself. The blood is always darker than I expect, a theatrical crimson that refuses to clot. By the time I remember hydrogen peroxide, it has dried into a outline of whatever country I was trying to draw.

Yesterday I watched a man on the train peel an orange in one continuous spiral. The rind landed in his lap like a neon snake. When he caught me staring, he smiled and held it up—“For luck,” he said, and dropped the coil into my palm. I carried it to the office, where it sat on my desk releasing small bursts of Mediterranean sunlight every time the air conditioning sighed on. At lunch I unfolded the spiral and counted the segments: thirty-two, same as the years I’ve been alive. I arranged them into a clock face, photographed it, then ate the orange slice by slice while answering emails. The rind dried into a brittle wreath that looks, if you squint, like a coronation scene enacted by citrus. I have not thrown it away. I keep rotating it a few degrees each hour, a private zodiac.

There is a crack in the sidewalk outside the library shaped exactly like the Iberian Peninsula. Every time I pass it I experience a tiny colonial impulse: drop a flag, claim it for the kingdom of whoever I am that day. Instead I plant something small—once a sesame seed, once a bead from a broken necklace, once the tear-off strip of a parking ticket. I think of these as undercover colonies, sleeper cells of meaning waiting for rain. One day a dandelion will grow with tiny iron balconies, and the librarians will chalk it up to wind.

I used to think adulthood would feel like entering a cathedral: vaulted certainties, stained-glass convictions. Instead it is more like standing in a laundromat at midnight watching socks orbit. You can identify your own garments by the holes, but everyone’s underwear uses the same gravitational law. Sometimes a stranger’s bra strap gets tangled with your T-shirt, and you spend ten minutes disentangling futures you never intended to touch. You leave with your clothes clean but never quite dry, the faint dampness of regret chilling your back as you walk home past neon signs that buzz like trapped wasps.

At night I listen to the neighbors argue about the dishes. Their kitchen window is level with my bedroom, separated by the narrow airway where satellites go to rust. She says, “You never listen,” and he says, “I’m listening now,” and she says, “That’s exactly the problem.” The dialogue is unoriginal, but the ache underneath is primary color. I could transcribe it and win a poetry contest, though I suspect the prize would be sleeplessness. Instead I pretend their words are a duet emanating from a radio in 1947, all warm static and big-band forgiveness. In that version, the plates stack themselves, the sink refills with cool moonlight, and nobody goes to bed tasting porcelain dust.

I read once that astronauts returning from long missions often forget, for fractions of a second, that gravity exists. They drop a cup and are stunned when it explodes. I think of this when I remember the unreturned email, the friend who became a pronoun, the birthday I keep re-adding to next year’s calendar as if time were a library book I can renew indefinitely. The cup keeps falling in some parallel corridor, shattering in slow motion, shards tumbling like mirrored confetti. Somewhere a janitor with infinite patience sweeps the pieces into a dustpan labeled “Soon.”

This morning the coffee finished dripping at the exact moment the sun cleared the neighbor’s gable. The kitchen filled with gold so thick I could have buttered bread with it. I carried the cup to the table, sat down, and began typing, not a novel, not even a paragraph, just a list:

Things that are lighter at night: bread, breath, the letter I keep meaning to write.
Things that are heavier at dawn: rain that hasn’t fallen yet, the pause before your name, the exact color of 5:47 a.m.

I got as far as item thirty-two—same as the orange segments—before the alarm on my phone reminded me I have a body that must be walked to the bus stop. I left the list on the screen, a half-built bridge swaying over an imaginary ravine. Tonight I’ll add another rung, maybe two. Eventually I’ll walk across and find myself on the other side, in a country whose flag is still drying, whose anthem is the sound of a house breathing while its inhabitant decides whether to answering the calling day or let it ring.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
